rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical difluoromethylation of acrylamides using CF2HSO2NHNHBoc, giving alkene hydroxydifluoromethylation with secondary N-aryl acrylamides and olefinic C-H difluoromethylation with tertiary acrylamides.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the sulfonyl hydrazide reagent (CF2HSO2NHNHBoc, 1) generates a diazene intermediate that decomposes to release SO2, N2, CO2, tBu• and the key •CF2H radical; a second anodic oxidation converts the carbon radical adduct to a carbocation; cathode evolves H2.

key_intermediates.name: diazene from sulfonyl hydrazide oxidation (I, CF2HSO2N=NBoc)
α-carbonyl benzylic C-radical from CF2H addition to acrylamide (I-adduct)
aza-oxyallyl cation IV (equilibrium tautomer of IIIA)

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes sulfonyl hydrazide 1 (2e−, −2H+) to diazene I, which fragments to •CF2H; the radical adds to the acrylamide β-carbon, the resulting α-carbonyl C-radical is oxidized at the anode to carbocation II/III, which either is trapped by H2O (secondary amides, via aza-oxyallyl cation IV → α-hydroxy amide) or undergoes Z-selective proton elimination (tertiary amides → olefinic C-H difluoromethylation product); protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: H2-18O (18O-labeled water as cosolvent)
probe_reagents.observation: [18O]-3 obtained in 40% yield with 79% 18O incorporation, confirming the hydroxyl oxygen in the alkene difunctionalization product originates from H2O.

probe_reagents.name: tertiary N,N-diisopropyl acrylamide 31 (substrate probe)
probe_reagents.observation: Under alkene-difunctionalization conditions, tertiary acrylamide 31 gave only C-H functionalization product 25 (63%) with no hydroxylated product, demonstrating that amide N-substitution controls cation fate (H2O addition vs proton elimination).

notes: Divergence between alkene difunctionalization (secondary N-aryl amides) and C-H functionalization (tertiary amides) is rationalized by equilibrium of α-amido cation IIIA with aza-oxyallyl cation IV, which favors nucleophilic addition of water over proton loss. The Z-selectivity in the C-H functionalization pathway is rationalized by proton elimination via conformer IIIB1 (favored) rather than the sterically encumbered IIIB2. No radical traps (TEMPO/BHT), CV, EPR, or DFT reported.
